specification.topic-statement: The credential loader reads API keys and secrets from a JSON configuration file and from environment variables, applying environment variables as overrides over file-based values.

scope.in-scope: Configuration file discovery and path resolution, JSON parsing and field extraction, environment variable override, credential retrieval by name, and all error handling along each path.
scope.boundary: Credential values are passed to provider and tool implementations that consume them. Whether a credential value is valid for its intended service is determined at connection time by those implementations, not here.

behavior.configuration-file-discovery:
When no explicit file path is provided, the path is derived from the environment. If the `IKIGAI_CONFIG_DIR` environment variable is set, the configuration file is sought at `/credentials.json`. Otherwise, the default path is `~/.config/ikigai/credentials.json`.
A path beginning with `~` is expanded by replacing the tilde with the value of the `HOME` environment variable. If `HOME` is not set and the path begins with `~`, loading fails and an error is returned. Paths not beginning with `~` are used as-is.
When an explicit file path is provided by the caller, that path is used directly, subject to the same tilde expansion rule.
behavior.file-loading:
If the resolved file path does not exist (as determined by a filesystem stat), loading continues without error and all credential fields from the file remain unset. A missing file is not an error.
If the file exists, it is parsed as JSON. If parsing fails, the failure is silently discarded and loading continues as though the file were empty. All credential fields from the file remain unset in this case.
If parsing succeeds but the JSON root is not an object (e.g., it is an array, a string, a number, or null), the file contents are discarded silently and all file-sourced fields remain unset.
If the JSON root is an object, each of the nine recognized field names is looked up. For each field, if the JSON value is a non-empty string, the credential is set to that string value. If the JSON value is absent, is not a string type, or is an empty string, the credential field remains unset. No other JSON types (numbers, arrays, booleans, null) are accepted as credential values.
A corrupted or invalid credentials file produces no error visible to the caller. Credentials that would have come from the file are simply absent, while environment variable overrides (applied in the next step) still take effect.
behavior.environment-variable-override:
After the file is processed, each of the nine credentials is independently checked against its corresponding environment variable. If the environment variable is set and its value is a non-empty string, that value replaces whatever was loaded from the file for that credential. If the environment variable is unset or set to an empty string, the file-sourced value (if any) is preserved unchanged.
The nine environment variable names and their corresponding credentials are: `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_API_KEY`, `BRAVE_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_SEARCH_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_SEARCH_ENGINE_ID`, `NTFY_API_KEY`, `NTFY_TOPIC`, and `IKIGAI_DB_PASS`.
Setting `OPENAI_API_KEY` to an empty string does not clear or override a file-sourced value. Only a non-empty environment variable value constitutes an override.
Environment variables override individual credentials, not all of them. A mix of file-sourced and environment-sourced credentials is the normal result when only some environment variables are set.
behavior.credential-retrieval:
An individual credential is retrieved by supplying its environment variable name as a key. The returned value is the credential string if set, or null if the credential is absent. If the supplied name does not match any of the nine recognized names, null is returned.
No validation of the credential value's format or content is performed at retrieval time.
behavior.no-format-validation: Credential values are stored and returned exactly as read from their source — no format checking, length validation, or structural verification is applied. A credential value of any non-empty string is accepted. Whether the value is a valid key for the intended external service is determined by that service when the credential is used.
